children.text: All right.
There we go.
Just start.
This is Nicholas Reyna. Today is June, it's 19th, right?
I think so.

June 19th, 2024. And I am here in Lukin, Texas for an interview with one of our community members. Before we begin, I'd like to get verbal consent for the form that we went over. Could you please say, I consent to being interviewed and audio recorded for this study?
Yes.
To get started, can you tell me about how you came to live in this community?
Born here, raised here. Uh, finished school here, and went to college at SFA. So I've, this has been my area. This is where I've been.
And could you tell me a bit more about this community? How would you describe it to someone who doesn't live here

Today? Uh, I think it's a good place to live. Uh, I think like every other place that's got its problems, and they're probably more pronounced today than they were back when I was a kid, but, uh, I think it's close enough to the big city that you can go and enjoy the things of the big city, but you still be able to stay in a, have a small town flavor to where you live.
And how do you think this community has changed throughout your time living here?
Throughout what? Throughout
Your time living here?

It's changed dramatically. When I was a kid, nobody locked their doors. This may be true for, I think, all over the United or many places in the United States, not necessarily the South and here in Lufkin, but they're just, n neighbors took care of neighbors. If someone told you, um, something you could rely on it. My grandpa caps used to say, if, uh, that fellow tells you that a rooster dipped snuff, you can look under his wing and find his snuff box, because it would be the truth. And, uh, I think, uh, I, I, I think the biggest thing that's changed is the family unit in many cases. I think, um, that's the, that's the sad part. I think that's, that's really, um, to have a mother and father. I never realized that where I'm headed with this is I had a wonderful, wonderful parents. They were disciplinarians, and I had no problem with that. Didn't hurt me a bit.

And, but as my son, my youngest was in high school, he had some friends who didn't, who didn't have a, a two parent household. And, um, until I met those kiddos and kind of got involved in that and involved in the, uh, athletics at Lufkin High School and the, the head coach here, and I got to be good friends. And some of those kids, you don't realize, I didn't realize, um, that I wish there was some way to fix that.
And what would you say are the strengths of this community?

Strength of the community. It's a can-do community. Um, you call on people to do something. Uh, you ask for something. I think this can best be described as there was a time that, uh, uh, the last bank I worked for the headquarters was in Nacodoches. The bank is over 120 years old. It's been here forever. Been over there forever. But I worked and managed a branch here in Lufkin. And so I worked closer to those folks. And one of the guys was telling me one day, he said, you know, the difference between Lufkin and Nacodoches? And I said, well, I know a lot of differences. And he said, he said, you know, um, we built an expo center over here, and then Lufkin steps up and builds one. We could set ours inside of. And so anything this community needs, like a community center, a civic center, an ex post, whatever, they make it happen. And I ran into this and found this to be true. Um, I was the, uh, president and the secretary for the high school booster club, athletic booster club. And, um,
if we had a project, all we had to do was go talk to some of the, the businesses in town, and we'd get it done. It is just a can do place.
And on the other hand, what would you say are some of the weaknesses?

annotation.text: Uh, you know, I've never thought of it that way because I kind of lived in a bubble

, and I, I don't see probably, I think there's probably more crime now than, than there was in the past. Mm-hmm. And I think that's true everywhere, but especially true here. And, uh, I'll go off on a tangent here a little bit. You may want to draw me back in. But in 1972, I was dating a, uh, a pretty girl, and she was a freshman at SFA, and I was, had my first banking job. And

Long story short, um, didn't, never occurred to me ever in 1972 that this would happen to us. And so I never would've dreamed that would've happened back then, more likely now. So they're bad people, and there's good people, and, uh, always have been, always will be. That's just the way it's,
Could you tell me about the roles and relationships you have in this community?
Uh, the, what
The roles and relationships you have in this community?

annotation.text: The, the roles mm-hmm. That I have in the community. Mm-hmm. Well, being retired, I don't do what I used to do, but I

, I, uh, at one time, coach Lilly baseball. I was in the, uh, lions Club. I was in the Kaas Club. It's expected of you when you work at a bank to be involved. I was, I was on committees with the Chamber of Commerce. And, uh, but as when I retired, I, uh, retired

. I thought, I've coached enough league baseball. I've done, I've done what I, you know, need to do. So I still, you know, somewhat active in church and those kind of things. But, but as far as, uh, being a treasurer or board person, uh, they ask me here, the, a while back, if I wanted to be on our local water board, I used to be the president. I went, I thank you. No, that job doesn't pay well. So I Good.

children.text: And I know you've already mentioned it a few times, but if you wanna elaborate or skip this question, um, what do you do for work?

What did I do for work? Mm-hmm. Well, throughout my career, I did a little bit of everything, but at the time of my retirement, my, my main focus was managing the second, or other than the main office. Mine was the biggest branch. I had 13, 14 employees, and I was responsible for the profitability of the branch. But in addition to that, and I was given, uh, goals and, uh, as far as production on loans, I was given goals is to deposit growth. And, uh, you had to meet and clear those hurdles. Uh, and, uh, the part I, you may get to this later, but the part I really, I didn't care much about doing budgets, and I didn't care budget for personnel issues. What I liked was talking to people. What I liked was, uh,

uh, seeing somebody get a house that they had worked hard for and saved. And I felt like I was part of, uh, being able to see them move into a home. I also did, but I also, one of my, but primarily I was called a commercial lender, but it, I still had customers that I had helped with car loans, and they still expected to see me do that, but I, those, that's the part of the job I really liked was, was the people. I miss that.
And how do you think your connections in this community influenced your work as a banker?
Gimme that one again.
How do you think think your connections in this community influenced your work as a banker?

My connection in the community? Mm-hmm. That's a good question, because you typically are, from my point of view, when you think of somebody that works at a bank, you typically think of somebody that likes to play golf. And somebody that may live in the, uh, crown colony that, uh, that likes to go to, uh, uh, cocktail evenings. And I'm a country guy. I do not like living you. You can see where I'm at. I'm, my house is here in the woods. I never played golf. I hate it. I don't hate it. I don't, I tried it once. I didn't like it. It seemed like it was a waste of time to me. I didn't, um, But so I knew that, I knew that I didn't, if you wanna call it hobnob, I didn't hobnob with a lot of doctors.

I had some attorney buddy, my best friend's, an attorney. I had some people that I knew. I, uh, the, the local fellow that owned all of McDonald's was a good friend of mine recently passed away. And, uh, but the, recently he was my friend is I used to fool with classic cars, and he did too. And one of my friends, uh, from school was, uh, restored cars. And so from that, we, we became, became good friends. Anyway, uh, I felt like my strength was that if it was a doctor, I could talk. And I had some doctor clients don't misunderstand, but my boss asked me one time or told me he, he had, we were talking about what he wanted, and he said, I don't you, I would like to see more of a mix and see in your portfolio and in your deposit base for that branch. I would like to see more medical people. And I had a handful. And I said, if that's what you say, that's what I'll do. I said, but you know what? I'd rather have,

this is what I told him. I said, I'd rather have 10 good old boys that work at the foundry than one doctor. He said, why? And my answer was, because those 10 guys will be loyal to you. They don't beat you up for rates. They don't expect you to wait on 'em and drop what you're doing and go cater to them. And I guess probably I can relate to them better than I do doctors, though.
Now I'd like to talk more about your work as a banker. Could you tell me about how you got into that role?

I, yeah, it's a pretty good story. When I was, I went to SFA and, uh, Can I digress a minute here? And I'll tell you something that, that, and this, I don't wanna get into politics, but my father came back from World War II with tuberculosis. He did a lot of stents in the VA hospital. He did nine months once, six months, once, three months, once. And because of that, we didn't have a terrible lot of money. Now, we had, we never went without. But, uh, when it came time to look at college, I would've liked to have gone to the University of Texas. And to be honest, I don't know if I was smart enough to get in, but there was no money for us. There was nothing. And at that point in time, there were no, I mean, this is 1965. There was, you know, I didn't, if, if there was any grants or anything, I knew nothing of it. But here's the plan. My dad worked 45 years at the paper mill here. They had a summer work program.

annotation.text: And he said, uh, and I could make enough money working at the paper mill in the summers to pay my tuition and buy my books at SFA. Both semesters I had to live at home, and I worked part-time pumping gas. And the only reason I mentioned this is because if you wanna do something, you can do it. You just gotta figure out a way to make it happen sometimes. But so what happened then? And the Vietnam War was going on, and, uh, I was motivated

to try to stay in school because you could get a deferment because the draft was going on. And then when they finally tried to draft me, uh, I, I didn't pass the physical, but, uh, it's another story for another time. But, uh, so I came, I, I got home. And, uh, here's the answer to your question. I had a buddy, a good buddy in high school who was working as a manager at a finance company. And, uh, he, when I got, came back from not passing the physical,

children.text: he and I were out messing around. He said, Hey, what are you gonna do? And I said, I'm not sure. I'm not sure at this point. He said, Hey, I know where there's an opening at a finance company. And I said, it is Plains Finance. I don't know if it's even still here, but, or around, but, uh, long story short, um, I applied for the, the position, and I went to work for them as a, as a collector of past dues. And, and, no, no, he worked for Plains Finance. Let me straighten this up. And I went to work for Home Credit Corporation, HCC Credit, and they made loans up to $1, 500, and they made the smaller loans to folks and charged an exorbitant constraint and that sort of thing. But, uh, um, but they had a really good trainee program. They had a good trainee program relative to collecting past dues. And you didn't get promotions if you didn't pass these, uh, uh, programs that they put forth, and if you wanted to be promoted. And, and, uh, so anyway, it was a good, it, it, it, it was a good fortunate thing for me. One day though, I was, went to lunch

and I had a buddy that I commuted with, uh, one semester over to s FFA that I went to school with. Good friend of mine ran into him at lunch. We were eating at Dairy Queen, and he went, he was working at the old Lukin National Bank that's not here now. And he said, uh, he said, Hey, uh, I'm being promoted and my job's gonna be open if you ever think about working for a bank. And I went, well, yeah, it crossed my mind one time, but I never, and, uh, so I came into Lu National Bank as a trainee, and I worked as a teller. I worked in new accounts, I worked all over the bank, which was really great. Um, and, and it gave me a good, uh, background. And from there, uh, I, I, I went to another bank and got into lending, got all along platform, and went from there.
And how long did you work, or how long were you working?
Uh, I worked 40, 45 years.
And what did a typical work day look like for you?

annotation.text: Again, I may ramble on you a little bit,

, but here's one of the things that appealed to me about going to work for a bank back in 19 69, 70, no, 70, excuse me, 71 those days, no, banks were open on Saturday. Many banks closed at two in the afternoon. Some, some at three. Now, you didn't get to go home at two or three o'clock, but the doors were closed. And what you did was you balance your drawer and you got everything ready for the next day, and you went home. And I thought, yeah, this is my kind of deal,

. And, uh, uh, but then banks started opening on Saturday, the hours changed. And, uh, uh, at the, uh, at the end of the, at the end of the, uh, my, my career, uh, we were open. Um, we were open five and a half days a week.

I didn't, I didn't have to work on Saturday. I, now, what I had did do was I had, uh, I had two other loan officers that worked under me, and we rotated, and we had to be on call in case the tellers had a problem. But, uh, but usually, um, I would be home by 5, 5 30. They been there sometimes. And, and I asked my boss when I interviewed for the job, um, I, at the end of the interview, never will forget. I said, I've got one more thing. He said, what's that? I said, I never asked to get off and don't play golf. I don't hunt. I fish a little bit, but I'm not asked to be off of that. But what I do like is luck in high school football. And, um, what I would like is sometimes when there's not a town game, I'd like to leave early. He said, I don't care if you leave all day, as long as you got your job done. I said, okay. So that, that sold me all that
. Um, but yeah, that, if that, that answered your question, that's what we did. That's what,

children.text: What does it mean to be a banker in this community?

annotation.text: Um, Even today, I ran into somebody here not long ago at a restaurant, and sadly I didn't recognize it, but that's not unusual because with bankers, here's the deal. The people you get to know really well are the ones who struggle
because you're always talking to 'em, the ones you see every year or every other year, and all they come in to do is make a loan. I know, I know

, but I didn't have a lot of interaction with those folks. And this guy, this guy and his wife came and he said, my cap, where have you been? And he shook my hand and he said, how are you? He said, and he looked at his wife, said, this guy made me my first loan when I was 18 years old, and I have followed him to everybody. That's cool. I that, uh, to think that you had an impact on somebody, much like coaching little league ball. I still have some, they're not kids anymore.

children.text: But when it's my birthday, I've still got some of those guys that are now almost 50 years old that will send me a birthday. We say, coach, I hope you have a great birthday. So it's the relationships that last, uh, through the years and the things that you felt like you had a part of the, uh, of their success that you, not you personally, the bank. Uh, I, but I was representative of the bank. And so if you help folks, that's the part, that's the part.
What aspects of your job bring you the most joy or satisfaction?

Uh, I've already touched on that. Uh, um, I'll tell you a quick story. I worked, uh, at a little bank at one point, and this lady came in who had rheumatoid arthritis. And she had one son that was special, but he could drive. She had another one that was wheelchair bound. And this lady, uh, I don't know how she could function, but she came in and she said, I, I need to borrow $500 to build a wheelchair ramp at my mobile home for my son in the wheelchair. So I said, you bet. I pulled everything up and put it together. I don't think she had any credit. Consequently, it was with outside my lending authority, if you don't have any credit or if you got that credit. But I went to the president of the bank. It was a great guy. He's gone now. He was deceased named Jim Hunt. And I said, here's this lady. I said, he said, you tell her

that the bank will pay for it. We'll just do that. And he said, no, wait a minute. He said, you make, make her a loan. Charge her a reasonable rate, like 10%, which is nothing on a, you don't make anything on $500 at 10%. Mm-hmm. And if she pays it, she'll bill some credit and she may need something else down the line, and then you'll be in a situation, or she'd be in a situation ever. As it turned out, um, she paid like a, just like nobody's business. And I did back her more loans, and she did get credit established, and she had some credit. And in, in the course of this, um, this lady developed some cancer on her nose, had to have her nose amputated. I mean, it was just one thing after another. Uh, but she was always up. And she came in

annotation.text: and she said, uh, the paper mill she lived in, behind the paper mill on some property, her property in a mobile home. She said, they're buying up property around us, and they've made me an offer, pretty good offer, but I wanna know if I can buy a house. And I found one, I won. I said, uh, absolutely. And so we, uh, just, she was so happy to get those kids and I, and as luck as as, and things would have it, the house was just down the road from where I live,

. Uh, but that lady passed away shortly after and did not get to enjoy it. That that was the saddest part. But to see somebody who was fighting to make the banking is one of the few professions that you, you can have an impact, I think, on things like that and helping people. And, uh, you know, while I'm talking about this, you know, one of the things that

children.text: banks, banks are regulated in on many fronts. Everybody thinks in terms of sound, uh, safety and soundness. And, and that's where the, they come in and check your loans. But there's a lot of other audits by the government, and there's other regulations. One of 'em is called the Community Reinvestment Act. And the, and, uh, uh, the Community Reinvestment Act says Bank, you must do some things in your community to benefit using your banking knowledge, using your skills to help people who are low income. So we look for things. And if you do not, if you don't score well, you can even be monetarily penalized when you're audited on this. And if you want to build branches, you're not gonna be able to build branches, you're not gonna be able to make acquisitions, nothing. So it's pretty important. They're very serious about it.

One of my customers was warden at the prison down in DA Hall, and, uh, he and I were talking about things one day, and he's actually, I'll give him the credit. He said, Hey, we, our prison is the last stop for these guys. They're gonna be getting out, uh, within two years, most of them or a year, and they required to complete a life skills class. He said, would you come talk to those classes about what's changed in banking? I said, you bet. That's perfect. I mean, they're low to moderate income. And, uh, I, I enjoyed going, I mean, the first time I went to, to go through all the gates and through the bars, you're out in the yard with them, and, and then you get in there. But it, I was, and you look at the class, and they would be sometimes 15, sometimes 20

people in the class. You look at one guy and he looks like the kid next door looking at another guy, and he looks like your grandfather. You know, you can't ask 'em why they're there. If they volunteer, that's fine. But there's always, there was always, almost without exception, there were two duds, maybe three that would just put their heads down, go to sleep, didn't care, don't want to hear. And, and I would talk to 'em about how to open new accounts, how to get your credit, credit reestablished. I would, I would talk to 'em, if you're going to thinking about doing a business, here's what you need to do. You have questions. And there would be four or five that take notes and have questions. But I felt like that, um, if, if some of that helped, some of those guys, 'cause they got a law, they got a steep hill to climb. And, and so that's the kind of things people don't know that, that banks do that. Uh, and,

and we also, uh, I went and, uh, I used to have a talk and I would go about every year and talk to, uh, kids in high school. And it was about banking primarily, but I also worked in, uh, one of my deals is, kid, please, kids don't drink and drive. I mean, here's what it can cost you. Stuff like that. Anyway, that, that, that was a long answer, but it, it, it, uh, uh, banking afforded the opportunity for me to, to feel like I was doing something good for somebody. Much like Little League Baseball. I loved being with those kids. And, and, and it just, I had one kid in particular who I started with him when he was six. I moved up to the next level. I got him again, and he had a temper. And, and

annotation.text: to this day, or when he got on up, he would ride a bicycle about five miles just to come see me,

. Now he'd come see me to see if I'd give him a beer, and I'd go, no, I'm not gonna give you a beer. But, uh, uh, but anyway, that's, uh, and you make good relationships with people there too. I had people come bank with me because you just, for me too, somebody does get something good for my kid. You, you got me. I'm paying attention. I'm, you know, so anyway,

And what are the biggest challenges you faced in your work? The biggest challenges? Mm-hmm. Today are at any point, uh, if we're talking today, having attained my age, you've learned, I've learned a lot of things the hard way. I have seen a lot of things. I can see how a mole hill can turn into a mountain. I can see how you can get blindsided. So I, if there was, if there was an Olympics for worrying, I, I would be, uh, first place most of the time, particularly about my children, I don't want them making the same. And I've got one that just doesn't listen, never will
. But, um,

one of the hardest things That I've learned is I, I can't believe that I'm this old. That's the first thing. My brain, my, I can't get it in my head. My brain still thinks I'm 30 years old, and it tells my body, oh, you can go cut that tree down and cut it up. And about halfway through my body goes, you lied, you lied. And so, so you, my thinking hasn't changed until I look in the mirror and I go, oh, man, this is an old guy,

. And so the, the hardest thing for me to do is remember I can't do the things I once did. I, you know, fool around and get hurt. And, uh, um, my challenge when I was working was, um, I, I'm, I'm kind of competitive and, uh,

children.text: if I wasn't meeting goals, and if the branch wasn't meeting goals, I just agonized over it. And That probably took years off of my life because I would, I would worry about it, because everybody wants to be a winner. You want to get, you get patted on the back. I had a, when I was at the bank that I told you about, where the lady we around in a loan meeting one morning, and one of the things they track for the loan officers is your percentage of past dues, percentage of loan. If you've got a portfolio of, of, I think mine when I retired, was approaching 40 million that I'd loaned out. That was out in books. Hmm. If your past dues that are nine that are 30 days, no, 60 to 90 days past or more

annotation.text: beyond, if the percentage of that total portfolio you wanted it, ideally you wanted it under 2%. Uh, this bank that I worked for, they were okay with 3. 5%. And, uh, of all the loan officers in that bank, mine were always, I was, my, my past dues were always, I was a little, so in this loan meeting, the president of the bank, Jim Hogan, he said, well, Mr. We got that through before we're done. He said, well, Mr. Caps, you, uh, you've had some attaboys today, you got anything you want to say? I said, I have been working at that time. I said, I've been working in the banks for 20 years, over 20 years, and one thing I've learned is that you can take a room full of attaboys and all it takes is one Oh, no. And they evaporate

children.text: He said, you're exactly right. And, uh, and, and that probably was one of the toughest things, because if you don't, if you don't, particularly with mortgage lending, it got so complicated. If you don't do the proper disclosures at the proper period of time and, and so forth, the bank is, you can cost the bank money monetarily. And you, you can't, there are certain things you can't, you gotta be careful that you don't mislead 'em. Uh, you know, I mean, unintentionally you would never do it, potentially. But anyway, it, it's, uh, it can be up from that standpoint, high pressure.

So. Alright. So now, uh, I wanna talk to you more about your experience as a resident in this community. For this first set of questions, I'll read a statement to you, and I'd like you to indicate the extent of your agreement with each one. If you give your answer on a scale of one to four, where one is completely disagree, two is somewhat disagree, three is somewhat agree, four is completely agree. If you'd like to comment on any statement, please feel free to,
Okay.
Right. The first phrase is, I consider myself to be an American.
Absolutely.
So a four,
Four, yeah.
Two. Using the same scale, how would you rate the following statement? I consider myself to be a Texan.
Four.

Which of the following three statements do you agree with most strongly? I'm American first and Texan second. I'm Texan first and American second, or being Texan and American are equally important to me.
I am a very proud Texan. So I think they're equally important. Huh.
The next phrase is to be a true Texan. You must know how to speak English. What would you rate this one using? The same scale we started with.
All right. Ask me that one again.
To be a true Texan, you must know how to speak English. What would you rate this one using? The same scale that we started with.
Hmm. I, maybe a three. I don't, you know, I don't think,

No, I don't think that's, I disagree.
So one with that.
Yeah. All right.
The next phrase is, in my community, you can do everything you want and get everything you need, even when you don't speak English.
Yes. Yes. Four. Four.
All right. Finally, in my community life has been pretty stable over the last 40 years. Things haven't changed much.
Uh, that's about a, two things have changed a lot.
Now I'd like to hear from you about the community itself. What are some of the things that you, what are some of the things that have changed in this community during the last few decades?

Well, the thing jumps to my mind to begin with is, uh, we had a paper mill. We had two foundries. We had a bunch of industry, and it's all gone for, for one reason or another. And, uh, uh, and we, we've turned into a, uh, medical hub for the surrounding areas. Nothing. That's great. And that's wonderful. But, uh, it was just really sad to see some of those businesses that had been here for a long time go away.
And overall, is this a good community to live in?
Uh, uh, four, yes.
Alright. And with this one, I think you could comment on all these. Um, what was, or is the best part about being a kid or a young person in this community?

annotation.text: The best part about being a teen. Well, not being a teen, I don't know

, but, um, um, I, I don't, I really don't, I don't know how to answer that. I, I think on the other side of the coin, I can answer. I wish there were more things and activities and to do. And, uh, I, I think, I think we fall short a bit there. And, uh, so, but I don't know. Uh, I, I, but, and, and I think that would eliminate a, a lot of the problems that some of the teens get into that, that, uh, that's, uh, sad to see. But anyway.

children.text: Do you feel that the traditional way of life in this community is going away? If yes, why do you think that's the case? If no, what do you think keeps things the same?
Well, I need to be, I need to define traditional. Are we talking like a household with mom, dad? Is that what we're talking, or what are we,
Um, I guess whatever you see as a traditional way of life or has been the traditional way of life here in this community.
I, I think it's, I think for the most part, yes. It's still, I, I see, uh, mostly, uh, mostly the same, I think. Hmm.
And why do you think that's the case?

annotation.text: Well, it's just from what I, what I observe. Well thank you so much for your answers. I really picked, really appreciate you taking time to talk with me. If you have the time, I just have one last request for you before we conclude the interview. One of the overarching research questions through the Home of Texas program is about linguistic changes in Texas. As a part of this project, a linguistic researcher is asking that interview participants read a brief passage, which should only take a couple of minutes. It's a short made up story about life in Texas that was created in the 1980s. And since then, linguistic researchers have recorded a few hundred Texans reading it. They use these recordings as samples of people's standard way of speaking as they try to track how speech in Texas varies and changes over time. Would you be willing to read this passage out loud?

annotation.text: Okay, let me see what you got. I mean, uh, let me see. Read. I don't hear very well.
. And, and, and you,

children.text: You done it?
Yeah. Alright,
Here's the passage.
Oh, that's what you, okay. So you, now let me be sure I understand. You want me to read this out loud or do you want me to, uh, read it out loud? Read it out loud? Mm-hmm.
Okay.

I've lived in Texas all my life. I was born in Titus County and when I was five we moved to a farm near White House, which is Southeast Tyler. I liked growing up in the country when my chores were done, I'd ride my horse climb trees or hike down to the creek to fish or swim. One time we tied a rope to a limb of a cypress tree and would swing out wide over the swimming hole and drop into the water. I'd pretended to be Tarzan swinging on a vine. In the spring, I'd fly kites and on summer nights we'd catch fireflies, but we called them lightning bugs. Once in a while we'd have fights with corn cobs or pine cones. In the winter, we'd build a fire, uh, in the fireplace and pop popcorn and roast peanuts and pecans. I guess I was a live wire When I was 19, I went to work in Dallas at a Firestone Tire store. I didn't like city life and for a long time I'd go home every chance I got. Mama would always cook my favorite food. Fried okra hop in John, that's rice and black-eyed peas and pecan pie.

We had lots of good times, but going home is not the same. Now after daddy died, mom sold the farm and moved to Tyler. I'd rather be living on a farm right now instead of here in the city. But my wife and kids don't understand me. When I gripe about city life and talk about moving to the country, my oldest child says, daddy, that's crazy. I'd just die if I had to live on a farm. I almost cried the first time she said that. I know Texas is changing, but I try to make sure we don't lose touch with our roots while we may live in a city. I want my kids to have a good feeling for the land, have a sense of place, and take pride in being Texans. If they lose our ties to the land, the pro progress is too high. Alright.

Thank you for contributing your voice to the collection and thank you so much for taking time to share your experience with me. I really appreciate your time and generosity. If you don't have any other questions or comments, I'll go ahead and stop the recording now.
